In Study 3, we aimed for a sample size of at least 343 participants. The minimum sample size results from a power analysis aiming for a power of .80 for a zero-order
correlation of .15 (the correlation of agency and advice taking we observed in Studies 1 and 2) given a Type I error level of .05. Our stopping rule for data collection was to stop inviting participants to the lab once we hit the required sample size but to include all participants who had already signed up for the study at that point, resulting in a total sample size of 351 participants.

The self-ratings of task-related expertise will be an index comprised of three items:
a)	the overall quality of their estimates on 7-point-likert-scales (1 = ŌĆ×very badŌĆ£, 7 = ŌĆ×very goodŌĆØ)
b)	the belief about the number of trials in which their own initial estimates were within 10% of the true values
c)	the belief about how they would rank among 100 hypothetical participants based on the accuracy of their estimates.
We will z-standardized these items (the third item will be reverse-coded) and aggregate them into a composite measure of participantsŌĆÖ self-perceived task-related expertise. 

The advice taking index (AT, Harvey & Fischer, 1997) will be calculated on each trial as follows: 
AT = (initial estimate - final estimate)/(initial estimate - advice)
We will then compute the mean AT scores per participants for both judgment tasks. The mean AT indicates the average percent weight participants placed on the advice. In line with previous research (e.g. Soll & Larrick, 2009), we will truncate the AT scores at 0 and 1 in order to prevent outliers from distorting the data. 

We will calculate confidence shifts as the difference between judgesŌĆÖ confidence in the accuracy their final estimate and their confidence in the accuracy of their initial estimates. Positive values of this measure indicate that judgesŌĆÖ confidence increased after receiving advice.

Since this is a linear transformation, it does not affect the relation between the variables of interest.

column72:
In accordance with the two original studies, we plan to do the following analyses:

1.	Zero order correlations of the following variables: agency, communion, mean AT score, mean confidence shift, rating of judgesŌĆÖ own expertise.

2.	A path model testing the effects of agency (H1) and communion on mean AT scores with participantsŌĆÖ ratings of their own expertise as a potential mediator of the agency effect (H2). We will use the R package lavaan to conduct this analysis and to infer the magnitude and 95% CI of the indirect effect.

3.	A logistic multilevel regression of the decision to adjust on agency and communion. In this model, we will treat agency and communion as level 2 fixed effects. The model will contain random intercept for participant and for the stimuli, but no random slopes since the model does not contain level 1 predictors.
column73:
Again, partici-
pants were resistant to advice weighting it far less than
50%, t(350) = 40.45, p < .001, and this pattern holds for
both judgment tasks individually, both t(350) < 31.32, both
ps < .001. Other than in the previous studies, the zero-order
correlation of agency and advice taking was not significant.
Neither was the correlation of communion and advice taking,
although this effect was close to statistical significance. Split-
ting this analysis by judgment task showed that the correla-
tion of agency and advice taking was absent in both tasks.
In contrast, there was a significant positive correlation of
communion and advice taking in the distance estimates. For
the weight estimates, the corresponding correlation was posi-
tive but not significant, meaning that we did not replicate the
unexpected communion effect observed in Study 2 (Table 4).

Analysis of confidence shifts
As in Study 1, our exploratory analysis of confidence shifts
revealed that participantsŌĆÖ confidence increased from their
initial to their final estimates (M = 3.44, SD = 1.07 vs.
M = 3.68, SD = 1.12), t(350) = 14.68, p < .001. As in Study
2, the confidence shifts were significantly related to commu-
nion, but this time, the correlation originated from the dis-
tance estimates and was in the opposite direction (Table 4).

Mediation analysis
We next tested for mediation of the effects of agency and
communion via participantsŌĆÖ rating of their own competence.
Because we did not measure ratings of advisor competence
in Study 3, the path model is somewhat simpler than those
in the previous studies. The resultsŌĆöboth across judgment
tasks and separated by judgment taskŌĆöare displayed in
Figure 3. Consistent with the analysis of the zero-order cor-
relations, the effect of agency on advice taking (c1) was not
significant, and this did not change when controlling for par-
ticipantsŌĆÖ ratings of their own and their advisorŌĆÖs expertise
(c10). The indirect effect of agency via ratings of judgesŌĆÖ
own competence emerged as it did in Studies 1 and 2. How-
ever, it was notably smaller, a1 ├Ś b = .029, p = .036, 95%
CI [.055, .002]. When analyzing the judgment tasks sep-
arately, there was only evidence of mediation for distance es-
timates, a1 ├Ś b = .04, p = .009, 95% CI [.07, .01], but
not for the weight estimates, a1 ├Ś b = .01, p = .228, 95%
CI [.04, .01]. In sum, the results provide at least partial sup-
port for the mediation hypothesis even when ruling out post
hoc justification as an alternative explanation.

Analysis of decision to adjust
As in the previous studies, we predicted the decision to adjust
towards the advice from agency and communion in a multilevel
logistic regression with random intercepts for the specific pairs
of cities nested within participants. As shown in Table 2, neither
agency nor communion predicted the decision to adjust. Be-
cause there was an interaction of communion and judgment
task, we ran separate analyses for the two judgment tasks. How-
ever, both models were consistent in that neither agency nor
communion predicted the decision to adjust, all zs < 1.46, all
ps > .145. Apparently, the interaction was due to a descriptively
larger effect of communion in the distance estimates as com-
pared with the weight estimates (b = 0.10 vs. b = 0.01).
Because agency and communion neither covaried with the
AT scores nor with the decision to adjust in Study 3, we de-
cided to omit the follow-up analyses we ran in the previous
studies. Absent the effects mentioned earlier, finding that
agency and communion are uncorrelated to the AT scores
when excluding trials, in which the judges chose to retain
their initial estimates, would be uninformative.

Accuracy of initial estimates
As in the previous studies, we investigated the correlation of
agency and judgesŌĆÖ initial accuracy measured as their MAPE
scores. Again, this correlation was negative but nonsignificant
when analyzing across tasks, r(351) = .07, p = .178. Sepa-
rate analyses by task revealed significantly lower initial
MAPE scores with increasing agency for distance estimates,
r(351) = .15, p = .005, but not for the weight estimates,
r(351) = .04, p = .426. Concerning communion, there was nei-
ther an effect when analyzing across tasks, r(351) = .04,
p = .485, nor when separating the analysis by task,
r(351) = .03, p = .513, and r(351) = .09, p = .089, for the
distance and weight estimates, respectively.

1.	Experimental Condition 1 will replicate the results of PK Study 3, in that women will report what other women ŌĆ£wantŌĆØ significantly more than what they ŌĆ£sayŌĆØ they want. We will test this prediction using a dependent (paired-samples) t-test comparing the average of the 15 items for column 2 (what women say) to the average of column 3 (what women want). The average for column 3 should be significantly greater than that in column 2. 
1.1.	Though not a central hypothesis, we also expect that, consistent with past research, across both Conditions, Column 1 averages of sexual intentions (what I want) will be significantly lower than both column 2 and column 3 (tested with separate dependent samples t-tests.)
2.	Experimental Condition 2 will replicate the results of PK Study 3, in that women will report what other women ŌĆ£wantŌĆØ significantly more than what they ŌĆ£sayŌĆØ they want. We will test this prediction using a dependent samples t-test comparing the average of the 15 items for column 2 (what women want) to the average of column 3 (what women say). The average for column 2 should be significantly greater than that in column 3. 
3.	The between-group comparisons between the mean of Condition 1 ŌĆ£what others actually wantŌĆØ and the mean of Condition 2 ŌĆ£what others actually wantŌĆØ will be significantly different. Specifically, the mean in Condition 1 will be higher than in Condition 2. We will test this with an independent groups t-test.
4.	The between-group comparisons between the mean of Condition 1 ŌĆ£what others sayŌĆØ and the mean of Condition 2 ŌĆ£what others sayŌĆØ will be significantly different. Specifically, the mean in Condition 1 will be higher than in Condition 2. We will test this with an independent groups t-test.
5.	The between-group comparisons between the mean of column 2 across Conditions 1 and 2 will not be significant. That is, people will respond with the same number to ŌĆ£What others wantŌĆÖ and ŌĆ£What others sayŌĆØ when they are presented first (in column 2) .We will test this with an independent groups t-test. column11:
A Gricean interpretation predicts that women responding to the ŌĆ£sayŌĆØ question first and women responding to the ŌĆ£wantŌĆØ question first would respond identically, as they would be indicating their beliefs about what other women want. Only when one question is asked after the other would women differentiate their responses to them. In contrast, if Perilloux and Kurzban are correct, question order should be irrelevant.
To investigate these competing possibilities, we conducted a preregistered experiment (see www.osf.io/5tejq/) with an online sample of women and varied the order in which Perilloux and KurzbanŌĆÖs questions were asked. We predicted that responses to the ŌĆ£sayŌĆØ and ŌĆ£wantŌĆØ questions would be equivalent when they were asked first, whereas Perilloux and Kurzban confirmed their prediction that ratings for the ŌĆ£wantŌĆØ question would be higher than ratings for the ŌĆ£sayŌĆØ question regardless of the order of the questions (personal communication, April 19, 2016). 
[...]
Simple-effects analyses revealed that this interaction was consistent with our preregistered hypotheses: As shown in Figure 1, ratings for the ŌĆ£sayŌĆØ and ŌĆ£wantŌĆØ questions were equivalent when these questions were asked first 
[...]
womenŌĆÖs differential responses to the ŌĆ£sayŌĆØ and ŌĆ£wantŌĆØ questions in Perilloux and KurzbanŌĆÖs study were driven by question-order effects and language conventions, rather than by womenŌĆÖs chronic underreporting of their sexual intentions

The focal preregistered analysis stated that we would compare individuals with socially engaged strategies such as ŌĆ£helping othersŌĆØ (n = 184) with individuals with other, nonsocial strategies, such as ŌĆ£stop smokingŌĆØ (n = 398). Overall, this analysis included 582 respondents (53.78% female) who were slightly younger on average (M = 46.87 years, SD = 14.53) compared with the first analysis. This analysis was registered after the SOEP IS data (2014 and 2015) had been collected but before the texts had been rated and before we looked at the life-satisfaction data. Thus, the variables necessary to run the focal analysis did not exist when the analyses and hypotheses were preregistered. Refer to the preregistration form and an additional timeline of the project on the OSF for details about the preregistration.

column72:
(1) Comparison of life satisfaction at baseline (t1) between the groups "socially engaged pursuit" and "individualistic pursuit" using an unpaired t-test.
Tests the hypothesis that individuals with socially engaged pursuit of happiness report higher levels of concurrent life satisfaction.

(2) t-test comparing the difference scores (life satisfaction t2-life satisfaction t1) between the two groups "socially engaged pursuit" and "individualistic pursuit"
Tests the hypothesis that the group of individuals with socially engaged pursuit experiences more positive changes in life satisfaction than the group with individualistic pursuit.

(3) Regression analysis predicting life satisfaction at t2 from life satisfaction at t1 and group variable ("socially engaged pursuit"/"individualistic pursuit").
Coefficient of interest is the effect of the group variable
Tests the hypothesis that, given the same life satisfaction at t1, an individual with a socially engaged pursuit experiences more positive changes in life satisfaction than an individual with individualistic pursuit.
column73:
In 2014, individuals who described a socially engaged strategy reported slightly higher levels of life satisfaction (M = 7.57) than individuals who described only nonsocial strategies (M = 7.29), d = 0.18, 95% CI = [0.01, 0.36], t(342.86) = ŌłÆ2.03, p = .043 (see Fig. 1b).

From 2014 to 2015, the group reporting a social strategy showed a slight increase in mean satisfaction of 0.19 scale points; d = 0.13, 95% CI = [0.08, 0.33], t(183) = 1.99, p = .047. By contrast, the mean life satisfaction in the group reporting nonsocial strategies remained virtually unchanged, with a decrease of 0.02 scale points, d = ŌłÆ0.01, 95% CI = [ŌłÆ0.15, 0.13], t(397) = ŌłÆ0.26, p = .792.

We ran a linear regression model predicting life satisfaction in 2015 from life satisfaction in 2014 and created a binary indicator reflecting social strategies (0 = only nonsocial strategies, 1 = at least one social strategy). The results indicated that, controlling for baseline differences in life satisfaction, socially engaged strategies had a positive effect on life satisfaction in 2015, b = 0.33, SE = 0.12, p = .005 (see Table S2 in the Supplemental Material for the complete regression output). This effect equals 0.21 SD units of the life-satisfaction scale, 95% CI = [0.06, 0.36].2

column31:
In 2014, 5,868 respondents (52.1% female) in 3,721 households participated in the SOEP-IS. The SOEP-IS spans the whole adult age range from 17 to 96 years (M = 51.79 years, SD = 18.31 years) and is a randomly drawn representative sample of private households in Germany. The sample consists of respondents with differing levels of education (19.0% low education: ISCED-Scores 0, 1, and 2; 56.2% medium education: ISCED-Scores 3 and 4; and 24.8% high education: ISCED-Scores 5 and 6; ISCED-1997; OECD, 1999), work situations (36.4% working full-time, 17.5% working part-time, 30.0% retired, 16.1% not working), and marital statuses (25.5% single, 56.7% married, 10.0% divorced, 7.8% widowed). In addition, 27.8% of the respondents live in households with children aged 16 years or younger.
[...]
We will include all SOEP-IS respondents that answered (1) the open-ended text question on ideas regarding life satisfaction (n = 1364), (2) the one-item measure of life satisfaction in 2014 (n = 6,638), and (3) the one-item measure of life satisfaction in 2015 (n = 5,897) in all analyses. Total sample comprised of 1,198 respondents.
[...]
We excluded those respondents that did answer the open-ended question about life satisfaction but did not report any active strategies how to maintain/increase their life satisfaction (see also the described coding procedure for the independent variable). This lead to the exclusion of 668 of the 1340 text answers; resulting in 672 valid answers which were coded for socially engaged pursuit of happiness.

column72:
(1) Comparison of life satisfaction at baseline (t1) between the groups "socially engaged pursuit" and "individualistic pursuit" using an unpaired t-test.
Tests the hypothesis that individuals with socially engaged pursuit of happiness report higher levels of concurrent life satisfaction.

(2) t-test comparing the difference scores (life satisfaction t2-life satisfaction t1) between the two groups "socially engaged pursuit" and "individualistic pursuit"
Tests the hypothesis that the group of individuals with socially engaged pursuit experiences more positive changes in life satisfaction than the group with individualistic pursuit.

(3) Regression analysis predicting life satisfaction at t2 from life satisfaction at t1 and group variable ("socially engaged pursuit"/"individualistic pursuit").
Coefficient of interest is the effect of the group variable
Tests the hypothesis that, given the same life satisfaction at t1, an individual with a socially engaged pursuit experiences more positive changes in life satisfaction than an individual with individualistic pursuit.
[...]
Because such a high number of participants did not report active strategies but instead answered in a different way, we intend to also explore whether there are any differences in level/change in life satisfaction between participants reporting an active strategy and participants reporting no active strategy.

In case we find no differences between the individuals reporting socially engaged pursuit of happiness versus other pursuits of happiness, we will pool those individuals and compare this "active" group with the group of respondents reporting no strategy.
In case we do find differences between the individuals reporting socially engaged pursuit of happiness versus other pursuits of happiness, we will not pool thes

p = .043 (see Fig. 1b).

We ran a linear regression model predicting life satisfaction in 2015 from life satisfaction in 2014 and created a binary indicator reflecting social strategies (0 =
only nonsocial strategies, 1 = at least one social strategy). 
[...]
Oster (2016) developed a method that allows researchers to assess the
extent to which an effect is robust to unobserved confounding on the basis of changes in coefficients and
changes in explained variance when controlling for
observed confounding. The procedure can be used to
answer the question of the extent to which unobserved
confounders would need to affect selection into the
group of treated respondents (i.e., respondents reporting socially engaged strategies) in order to negate the
observed effect.3
 This analysis indicated that the selection based on unobserved confounders would need to
be 1.47 times as important as the selection based on
the observed confounder (life satisfaction in 2014) to
render the effect of socially engaged strategies on life
satisfaction in 2015 zero, which can be considered a
robust effect according to the standard suggested by
Oster (2016). 
[...]
Therefore, we additionally ran an unpreregistered
mediation analysis to test whether the relationship between
strategies and life satisfaction could in part be explained by
socializing with friends, relatives, or neighbors.
[...]
Among the respondents who had
reported an active idea, those who reported a socially
engaged strategy in 2014 reported more frequent socializing (social: Mdn = 4; nonsocial: Mdn = 3, z = ŌłÆ3.46,
p < .001, or when we treated socializing as a continuous
variable, social: M = 3.42; nonsocial: M = 3.19; d = 0.32,
95% CI = [0.15, 0.50]), t(410.98) = ŌłÆ3.81, p < .001.
[...]
To test whether more frequent socializing could
partly explain the relationship between socially engaged
strategies and life satisfaction, we ran a mediation analysis in Mplus (Version 7.4; Muth├®n & Muth├®n, 2012). In
our model, we predicted life satisfaction in 2015 from
life satisfaction in 2014, whether or not respondents
had reported a socially engaged strategy in 2014 and
socializing in 2015 (treated as an ordinal variable). Furthermore, we predicted socializing in 2015 from the
strategies reported in 2014, and we allowed life satisfaction in 2014 to correlate with socializing in 2015 and
strategies reported in 2014. 
[...]
In addition to the central preregistered analysis, we ran
exploratory analyses including additional independent
variables (i.e., alternative categories of strategies) and
additional dependent variables (i.e., alternative measures
of well-being). Details and results can be found in the
Supplemental Material.
